food_subgroup: Baked tapioca starch coconut milk cookie

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Kuih Bangkit. Closest parent is tapioca-based coconut milk cookie/biscuit (FOODON:03301710 — baked product). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Baked tapioca starch and coconut milk cookie (Malay kuih bangkit style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CEK is the closest FoodEx2 parent for biscuits/cookies; no specific Kuih Bangkit code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Kuih Bangkit or tapioca coconut milk cookie as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Kuih Bangkit is 4 small moulded cookies weighing approximately 40g, delivering about 186 kcal, 28.8g carbohydrate, and a glycemic load of approximately 24.5.

nutrient_source.title: Kuih Bangkit — IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Kuih Bangkit. IMR Malaysia MyFCD is an approved primary regional source under Project Veritas v2 source policy for Malaysian dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Tapioca Starch-Based Products — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Kuih Bangkit exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 85 is estimated from roasted and baked tapioca starch products in the database, which consistently show GI values in the 80–95 range. Glycemic load per 40g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (85 × 28.8) / 100 ≈ 24.5. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: A 40g serving of Kuih Bangkit delivers only 18 mg sodium, well below the threshold of concern for hypertension management; sodium intake from this food is clinically negligible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~85) from roasted tapioca starch combined with high total carbohydrate (72g/100g) and high sugar (28g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: A single 40g serving produces a glycemic load of approximately 24.5, which is very high for a small cookie portion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit consumption, as eating 8–10 cookies (~80–100g) — a typical Hari Raya amount — generates a glycemic load of 49–58, posing significant postprandial glucose risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (14.5g/100g) from concentrated coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kuih Bangkit contains 14.5g saturated fat per 100g — among the highest of common Southeast Asian kuih — and a 40g serving delivers 5.8g saturated fat (approximately 29% of the 20g daily limit for a 2000 kcal diet), making regular or large-quantity consumption a significant cardiovascular risk factor.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and very high GI promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The combination of very high saturated fat (14.5g/100g) and very high GI (~85) in Kuih Bangkit promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this food only in very small quantities during festive occasions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kuih Bangkit is a delightfully delicate Malay and Peranakan festive cookie made from roasted tapioca starch, coconut milk, egg yolk, sugar, and fragrant pandan leaf extract, moulded into intricate decorative shapes — flowers, fish, and shells — and baked until just set. Its defining characteristic is its extraordinary melt-in-the-mouth texture: so light and airy that it literally crumbles and dissolves on the tongue the moment it is eaten. Despite its tiny size (each piece weighs approximately 10g), Kuih Bangkit is extraordinarily calorie-dense at 465 kcal per 100g, driven by the combination of highly concentrated roasted tapioca starch and the fat from coconut milk. The cookie is gluten-free and dairy-free, making it suitable for those with gluten intolerance, but it contains egg yolk and is not vegan. Nutritionally, it is very high in carbohydrates (72g/100g) and saturated fat (14.5g/100g), with a very high glycemic index of approximately 85 — among the highest of all common Southeast Asian kuih. A standard serving of 4 pieces (40g) delivers approximately 186 kcal, 28.8g carbohydrate, 7.4g fat, and a glycemic load of 24.5. Clinically, Kuih Bangkit poses significant concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high GI and carbohydrate density, and for those with cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat content from coconut milk. It is a beloved Hari Raya Aidilfitri cookie exchanged between neighbours and families during the festive season, and is also enjoyed during Chinese New Year celebrations in Peranakan communities across Malaysia and Singapore.
common_user_queries: kuih bangkit vs london almond cookie

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research Malaysia), which is an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using closely analogous roasted tapioca starch baked products with documented confidence of 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Kuih Bangkit not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this baked tapioca cookie not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD source; Kuih Bangkit contains no hydrogenated fats by traditional formulation. All core macros (protein_g 3.2, fat_g 18.5, carbohydrate_g 72.0) are non-zero and fully supported. GI of 85 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database roasted/baked tapioca starch products; no direct Kuih Bangkit GI measurement exists in the database. Vitamin A value (28 mcg RAE) reflects pandan leaf and egg yolk contribution as reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Hindi local name (कुइह बांगकित) is a transliteration; no established Hindi culinary name exists for this food.
